description:
Design and build Abacus: a standalone SQLite-backed graph tool that models an application's API surface, entities, pages, and user actions. Serves as the persistent, cumulative codebase understanding layer that bridges Gherkin specs to implementation. Standalone project (new Linear project: Abacus) with optional beads integration via hooks.

Linear issue: https://linear.app/pcom-mike/issue/ABA-1/brainstorm-abacus-living-application-graph-gherkin-driven-bdd

## Core Architecture
- SQLite database (.abacus/abacus.db)
- CLI tool (abacus or ab)
- MCP server for Claude Code integration
- Auto-scan scripts for structural layer
- Agent enrichment protocol for semantic layer

## Graph Node Types (Launch)
1. Routes — auto-scanned from oRPC contracts (method, path, input/output schemas)
2. Entities — auto-scanned from Prisma schema (models, fields, relations)
3. Pages — auto-scanned from React Router config (URL, components)
4. Actions — agent-enriched semantic bridge (gherkin_patterns, api_route, entities, page, permissions, ui_hints)
5. Permissions — hybrid (auto-scan ACL config + agent enrichment)

## The BDD Flow
Gherkin step → abacus actions --match → resolves to Action node → Action tells agent the API route, entities, page, and permissions → agent generates E2E test + implementation

## Population Strategy
- Hybrid: auto-scan + agent enrichment
- abacus scan rebuilds structural layer from code
- Agents enrich Actions as they work through features
- Actions are the cumulative knowledge — they persist and grow

## Beads Integration (optional)
- Hooks connect beads and abacus when both are present
- When a bead is worked on, agents can annotate which Actions/Routes were touched
- Graph data can feed into bead descriptions
title: Brainstorm: Abacus — Living Application Graph + Gherkin-driven BDD

description:
Build Abacus: a standalone SQLite-backed graph tool that models an application's API surface, entities, pages, and user actions. Serves as the persistent, cumulative codebase understanding layer that bridges Gherkin specs to implementation.

Linear project: Abacus (pcom-mike workspace)
Linear issue: https://linear.app/pcom-mike/issue/ABA-1/brainstorm-abacus-living-application-graph-gherkin-driven-bdd

## Architecture

- **Language**: Go (single binary, excellent compiler feedback, matches beads tooling)
- **Database**: SQLite (.abacus/abacus.db) per target project via modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go, no CGo)
- **CLI**: cobra (same as beads)
- **MCP Server**: github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (official Go SDK)
- **Graph Model**: Adjacency list (nodes + edges tables) with JSONB properties, FTS5 search
- **Node Types**: Route, Entity, Page, Action, Permission

## BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) Scanner Architecture

Abacus does NOT parse source code itself. Instead:
- Abacus defines a **Scanner Protocol** — a JSON schema for scan results
- Scanners are **standalone executables** that output JSON conforming to the protocol
- Abacus spawns scanners as subprocesses, reads their JSON output, ingests into SQLite
- Config (.abacus/config.yaml) maps scanner IDs to executable commands

This means:
- Abacus core is 100% Go — no TypeScript, no AST parsing, no language-specific deps
- Scanners can be written in ANY language (Node.js for TS, Python for Django, etc.)
- We ship 4 reference scanners as npm packages (oRPC, Prisma, React Router, Express)
- Community can write scanners for any stack

## Project Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── go.mod / go.sum
├── cmd/abacus/main.go           # CLI + MCP server entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── db/                      # SQLite connection, schema, migrations
│   ├── graph/                   # GraphRepository, ActionService
│   ├── match/                   # MatchService, CucumberMatcher, FuzzyMatcher, SuggestionBuilder
│   ├── scanner/                 # Scanner protocol types, subprocess runner
│   └── cli/                     # Cobra command definitions
├── mcp/                         # MCP server tool/resource registration
├── scanners/                    # Reference scanner implementations (Node.js)
│   ├── orpc/                    # @abacus/scanner-orpc
│   ├── prisma/                  # @abacus/scanner-prisma
│   ├── react-router/            # @abacus/scanner-react-router
│   └── express/                 # @abacus/scanner-express
└── testdata/                    # Test fixtures
\`\`\`

## Technology Stack
- Go 1.23+ (single binary distribution)
- modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go SQLite, no CGo)
- github.com/spf13/cobra (CLI framework)
- github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk (MCP server)
- github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go (pattern matching)
- github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go (Gherkin parsing)
- Node.js scanner packages: ts-morph, @prisma/internals, @cucumber/gherkin

## Key Decisions
1. Go for core — single binary, great compiler messages, matches beads ecosystem
2. BYOASTP — scanners are standalone executables, not embedded parsers
3. .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project (like .beads/)
4. Scanner Protocol is the interface — JSON in/out, language-agnostic
5. Match algorithm is a GROWTH mechanism — LLM fallback creates new Actions
6. 4 reference scanners ship as npm packages for the hourly stack
7. Official Go MCP SDK for Claude Code integration

## Phases
1. Foundation (scaffolding, schema, scanner protocol)
2. Scanners (oRPC, Prisma, Router, Express — Node.js, parallelizable)
3. Core Features (query layer, actions, match algorithm)
4. Interfaces (CLI commands, MCP server)
5. Integration (E2E validation against hourly codebase)

Key facts: (1) GOMODCACHE=/Users/mjn/workplace/abacus/.gomodcache for go commands, (2) Scanner packages at scanners/{orpc,prisma,express,react-router}/ — all npm test passing, (3) oRPC: 16 tests, ts-morph .route() chain parsing, (4) Prisma: 30 tests, regex-based model/enum/relation parsing with edges, (5) Express: 18 tests, router.get/post + route chaining + middleware extraction, (6) React Router: 14 tests, JSX <Route> parsing with nesting + protection detection, (7) Types duplicated across scanners — future: extract to @abacus/scanner-protocol, (8) Prisma scanner is gold-standard reference (cleanest separation, most robust error handling). Next action: run /beads-work on aba-zca.11 to implement CLI scan and query commands that wire up the Go core to these Node.js scanners.

title: Abacus: Living Application Graph + Gherkin-driven BDD

description:
## What

Set up the Abacus Go module with directory structure, cobra CLI skeleton, build tooling, and configuration schema. This is the foundation all other beads build on.

## Context

Abacus is a Go project producing a single binary. It uses cobra for CLI (same as beads), modernc.org/sqlite for the database, and the official Go MCP SDK. The .abacus/ directory lives in the TARGET project, not in the Abacus repo.

## Implementation

### Directory Structure
\`\`\`
abacus/
├── go.mod                          # module github.com/mjn/abacus
├── go.sum
├── Makefile                        # build, test, install targets
├── cmd/abacus/main.go              # entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── db/                         # SQLite layer
│   ├── graph/                      # Graph operations
│   ├── match/                      # Match algorithm
│   ├── scanner/                    # Scanner protocol + runner
│   └── cli/                        # Cobra commands
├── mcp/                            # MCP server
├── scanners/                       # Reference Node.js scanners
│   ├── orpc/
│   ├── prisma/
│   ├── react-router/
│   └── express/
└── testdata/                       # Test fixtures
\`\`\`

### go.mod
\`\`\`go
module github.com/mjn/abacus

go 1.23

require (
    github.com/spf13/cobra v1.9.1
    modernc.org/sqlite v1.37.1
    github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk v1.4.0
    github.com/cucumber/cucumber-expressions-go v0.0.0
    github.com/cucumber/gherkin/go/v38 v38.0.0
)
\`\`\`

### Makefile
- \`make build\` — \`go build -o bin/abacus ./cmd/abacus\`
- \`make install\` — \`go install ./cmd/abacus\`
- \`make test\` — \`go test ./...\`
- \`make lint\` — \`golangci-lint run\`

### Entry Point (cmd/abacus/main.go)
\`\`\`go
package main

import (
    "os"
    "github.com/mjn/abacus/internal/cli"
)

func main() {
    if err := cli.Execute(); err != nil {
        os.Exit(1)
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Configuration Schema (.abacus/config.yaml)
\`\`\`yaml
version: 1
project:
  name: "hourly"
  root: "."
scanners:
  orpc:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-orpc"
    options:
      contractPaths: ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
      handlerPaths: ["backend/src/orpc/**/*.handlers.ts"]
  prisma:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-prisma"
    options:
      schemaPath: "backend/prisma/schema.prisma"
  react-router:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-react-router"
    options:
      configPath: "frontend/src/App.tsx"
      pagesDir: "frontend/src/pages"
  express:
    command: "npx @abacus/scanner-express"
    options:
      routePaths: ["backend/src/routes/**/*.ts"]
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] \`go build ./...\` compiles without errors
- [ ] \`go vet ./...\` passes
- [ ] \`make build\` produces bin/abacus binary
- [ ] \`make install\` installs abacus to GOPATH/bin
- [ ] \`abacus --version\` prints version
- [ ] \`abacus --help\` prints help
- [ ] Config YAML parsing works for valid config
- [ ] Config parsing rejects invalid YAML with clear errors

## Validation

- [ ] Single binary builds in < 5 seconds
- [ ] Binary size is reasonable (< 30MB)
- [ ] All directories exist and Go packages compile
- [ ] No CGo dependencies (pure Go)

## Files

- go.mod, go.sum
- Makefile
- cmd/abacus/main.go
- internal/cli/root.go
- internal/config/config.go (config parsing)
- .gitignore

## Dependencies

None — this is the first bead.
title: Phase 1: Project Scaffolding & Configuration

description:
## What

Implement the cobra CLI skeleton with the \`abacus init\` command, global flags, and output formatting. Uses cobra (same as beads CLI).

## Context

Single binary entry point. Global flags: --json, --db, --quiet, --verbose. The init command creates .abacus/ directory, generates config.yaml by auto-detecting project structure, and initializes the SQLite database.

## Implementation

### Root Command (internal/cli/root.go)
\`\`\`go
var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "abacus",
    Short: "Living application graph for Gherkin-driven BDD",
    Aliases: []string{"ab"},
}

// Global flags
var jsonOutput bool
var dbPath string
var quiet bool
var verbose bool
\`\`\`

### Init Command (internal/cli/init.go)
- Create .abacus/ directory
- Auto-detect project type (look for Prisma schema, oRPC patterns, React Router config, Express routes)
- Generate .abacus/config.yaml with detected scanner commands
- Initialize SQLite database with schema
- Add .abacus/abacus.db to .gitignore

### Output Module (internal/cli/output.go)
- JSON mode: json.Marshal to stdout
- Human mode: tabwriter for aligned tables
- Quiet mode: suppress non-essential output

## Testing

- [ ] \`abacus --version\` prints version
- [ ] \`abacus --help\` lists all commands
- [ ] \`abacus init\` creates .abacus/ with config and DB
- [ ] \`abacus init --json\` outputs JSON summary
- [ ] Auto-detection finds project scanners
- [ ] Init on already-initialized project warns

## Files

- internal/cli/root.go
- internal/cli/init.go
- internal/cli/output.go
- internal/cli/init_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.2 (needs DB init)
title: Phase 4: CLI Foundation & Init Command

description:
## What

Implement cobra commands for scanning and querying: scan, routes, entities, pages, actions, graph, stats.

## Context

Each command file adds itself to rootCmd via init(). All commands support --json output. The scan command uses the Scanner Runner to spawn external scanner executables.

## Commands

- \`abacus scan [type]\` — run scanners, ingest results
- \`abacus routes [--match] [--method] [--tag]\` — query routes
- \`abacus entities [name] [--match] [--fields]\` — query entities
- \`abacus pages [--match] [--protected]\` — query pages
- \`abacus actions [--match] [--route] [--entity]\` — query actions
- \`abacus action create <name> --gherkin <pattern> ...\` — create action
- \`abacus graph <node-id> [--depth]\` — connected subgraph
- \`abacus stats\` — summary statistics

## Testing

- [ ] Each command registers and shows in --help
- [ ] scan runs configured scanners
- [ ] Query commands filter correctly
- [ ] --json produces valid JSON for all commands
- [ ] Empty graph handled gracefully

## Files

- internal/cli/scan.go
- internal/cli/routes.go
- internal/cli/entities.go
- internal/cli/pages.go
- internal/cli/actions.go
- internal/cli/graph.go
- internal/cli/stats.go
- internal/cli/scan_test.go
- internal/cli/query_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.10 (CLI foundation), aba-zca.4, aba-zca.5, aba-zca.6, aba-zca.16 (scanners), aba-zca.7, aba-zca.8 (graph + actions)
title: Phase 4: CLI Scan & Query Commands

description:
## What

Implement cobra commands for matching and coverage: match, coverage.

## Commands

- \`abacus match <step-text> [--keyword] [--create] [--threshold]\` — match single step
- \`abacus match --file <feature-file> [--scenario] [--create-all]\` — match all steps in feature file
- \`abacus coverage [glob]\` — coverage report across feature files

Uses cucumber/gherkin-go for feature file parsing and the MatchService for matching.

## Testing

- [ ] Single step matching works for all three tiers
- [ ] Feature file parsing works with real .feature files
- [ ] Coverage report shows correct percentages
- [ ] --create auto-creates Actions from suggestions

## Files

- internal/cli/match.go
- internal/cli/coverage.go
- internal/cli/match_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.10 (CLI foundation), aba-zca.9 (match algorithm)
title: Phase 4: CLI Match Command

description:
## What

Implement the MCP server using the official Go MCP SDK (github.com/modelcontextprotocol/go-sdk). Exposes the Abacus graph to Claude Code agents via stdio transport.

## Context

The MCP server is built into the same binary as the CLI: \`abacus mcp-server\` starts the MCP server on stdio. Uses the Go SDK's mcp.NewServer, mcp.AddTool, and StdioTransport.

Tool input schemas are auto-generated from Go struct tags + jsonschema annotations.

## Implementation

### Entry Point
\`abacus mcp-server\` cobra command starts the MCP server:
\`\`\`go
func init() { rootCmd.AddCommand(mcpServerCmd) }

var mcpServerCmd = &cobra.Command{
    Use:   "mcp-server",
    Short: "Start MCP server for Claude Code integration",
    RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
        return mcp.StartServer()
    },
}
\`\`\`

### Tool Registration (mcp/server.go)
\`\`\`go
func StartServer() error {
    server := mcp.NewServer(&mcp.Implementation{
        Name: "abacus", Version: "0.1.0",
    }, nil)

    // Register all tools
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_scan", ...}, scanHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_routes", ...}, queryRoutesHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_entities", ...}, queryEntitiesHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_pages", ...}, queryPagesHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_query_actions", ...}, queryActionsHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_create_action", ...}, createActionHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_update_action", ...}, updateActionHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_match_gherkin", ...}, matchGherkinHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_match_scenario", ...}, matchScenarioHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_graph_context", ...}, graphContextHandler)
    mcp.AddTool(server, &mcp.Tool{Name: "abacus_stats", ...}, statsHandler)

    return server.Run(context.Background(), &mcp.StdioTransport{})
}
\`\`\`

### Claude Code Configuration
\`\`\`json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "abacus": {
      "command": "abacus",
      "args": ["mcp-server"],
      "cwd": "/path/to/target/project"
    }
  }
}
\`\`\`

### Tool Input Types
Each tool handler has a Go struct with jsonschema tags:
\`\`\`go
type MatchGherkinInput struct {
    StepText   string  \`json:"stepText" jsonschema:"the Gherkin step text to match"\`
    Keyword    *string \`json:"keyword,omitempty" jsonschema:"Given, When, Then, or And"\`
    AutoCreate bool    \`json:"autoCreate,omitempty" jsonschema:"auto-create Action if no match found"\`
}
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] Server starts on stdio
- [ ] All 11 tools registered and callable
- [ ] Tool input validation via struct tags
- [ ] Query tools return correct filtered results
- [ ] Match tool returns correct tier results
- [ ] Create action tool validates and persists
- [ ] No stdout contamination (all logs to stderr)
- [ ] Server startup < 500ms

## Validation

- [ ] Configurable in Claude Code settings
- [ ] All tools callable from Claude Code
- [ ] Responses properly formatted
- [ ] Single binary — no separate MCP server install

## Files

- mcp/server.go
- mcp/tools.go (tool handlers)
- mcp/resources.go
- mcp/server_test.go
- internal/cli/mcp_server.go (cobra command)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.7, aba-zca.8, aba-zca.9, aba-zca.3
title: Phase 4: MCP Server

description:
## What

End-to-end integration test validating the full Abacus workflow against the real hourly codebase: init → scan (all 4 scanners) → query → action create → match → MCP tool calls.

## Context

The hourly codebase has:
- 45+ oRPC contracts, 30+ Express routes, 100+ Prisma models, 50+ React pages
- Existing auth/login.feature with 13 step definitions
- This bead proves the entire pipeline works together

## Test Suite

1. **Init & Scan**: abacus init on hourly, run all 4 scanners, verify node counts
2. **Idempotency**: scan twice, verify 0 added/removed
3. **Query**: routes --match auth, entities User --fields, pages --protected, graph traversal
4. **Action Enrichment**: create login Action with route/entity/page references
5. **Match Algorithm**: test all 3 tiers against real graph
6. **Feature Coverage**: parse login.feature, run coverage report
7. **MCP Server**: start server, call tools via Go MCP client
8. **Scanner Protocol**: verify all 4 scanner outputs conform to protocol v1

### Performance Benchmarks
- Init + full scan: < 15 seconds (includes Node.js scanner startup)
- Graph query: < 100ms
- Match: < 200ms
- MCP startup: < 500ms

## Files

- internal/e2e/integration_test.go
- testdata/hourly-fixtures/ (if needed)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.11, aba-zca.12, aba-zca.13, aba-zca.16
title: Phase 5: E2E Integration Test Against Hourly

description:
## What

Implement optional hooks that connect Beads issue tracking with the Abacus graph. When agents close beads, they can annotate which Actions/Routes/Entities were touched. Graph data can also feed into bead descriptions.

## Context

Per decision in aba-wyp: "Beads integration via optional hooks — composable, not coupled." This means:
- Abacus works perfectly without Beads
- If .beads/ is detected alongside .abacus/, hooks enhance both tools
- Hooks are post-processing — they never block the primary workflow

### Hook Types

**1. Bead Close Hook** (post-close)
When \`bd close <id>\` is called:
- Check if the bead's work touched any Abacus Actions
- If yes, log: \`bd comments add <id> "ACTIONS: action-1, action-2"\`
- Also update Action properties with bead reference

**2. Bead Create Hook** (post-create)
When \`bd create\` is called for a feature:
- Query Abacus for related Actions
- If found, inject context into bead description
- Helps agents start with graph knowledge

**3. Scan on Bead Close** (optional)
When a bead that modified code is closed:
- Trigger \`abacus scan\` to pick up any new Routes/Entities/Pages
- Report if the graph changed

### Implementation Approach
These hooks can be implemented as:
- Shell scripts in .beads/hooks/ that call \`abacus\` CLI
- Or as a small integration module in @abacus/core

Start with shell script hooks (simpler, composable, works with any bd version).

## Testing

- [ ] Bead close hook detects when Actions were referenced in work
- [ ] Bead create hook injects Abacus context when available
- [ ] Hooks are no-ops when .abacus/ doesn't exist
- [ ] Hooks don't block or slow down bd operations
- [ ] Scan-on-close detects new graph nodes

## Validation

- [ ] Hooks install correctly alongside existing beads hooks
- [ ] Integration is truly optional — removing hooks doesn't break anything
- [ ] Graph annotations on beads are useful for future reference

## Files

- packages/core/src/integrations/beads.ts (optional module)
- scripts/hooks/beads-post-close.sh
- scripts/hooks/beads-post-create.sh

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.11 (needs working CLI scan), aba-zca.8 (needs Action service)
This is a STRETCH goal — not required for MVP.
title: Phase 5: Beads Integration Hooks (Optional)

description:
## What

Implement the Express Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-express) that parses Express route files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON. This scanner captures the **primary backend route infrastructure** — Express is the actual server, not a legacy layer.

## Context

The hourly backend is an Express application. oRPC is used for contract definition and client SDK generation, but Express is the runtime route infrastructure. This means:

- **Express scanner** captures: actual route registration, middleware chains, auth guards, rate limiters, route mounting hierarchy, and routes that may not have oRPC contracts
- **oRPC scanner** captures: typed API contracts (input/output schemas, tags, summaries) used for client generation

Both scanners are essential and complementary. Together they give a complete picture:
- oRPC tells you WHAT the API contract looks like (types, schemas)
- Express tells you HOW the route is actually wired (middleware, guards, mounting)

The backend has 30+ route files in backend/src/routes/.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/express/
- Use ts-morph to find Express route patterns
- Extract: HTTP method, path, middleware chain, handler function name
- Handle route prefixes from app.use() mounting
- Node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{fullPath}\`

### Express Route Patterns to Detect
\`\`\`typescript
// Direct routes
router.get('/users', getUsers);
router.post('/users', validateBody, createUser);

// Parameterized routes
router.get('/users/:id', getUserById);

// Route mounting (prefix composition)
app.use('/api/v1', userRouter);  // all routes get /api/v1 prefix

// Route chaining
router.route('/users/:id')
  .get(getUser)
  .put(updateUser)
  .delete(deleteUser);

// Middleware stacks (capture these — they reveal auth, validation, rate limiting)
router.post('/admin/users', requireAuth, requireRole('admin'), rateLimiter, createUser);
\`\`\`

### Middleware Extraction
Middleware names are valuable context for Actions — they reveal:
- Auth requirements (requireAuth, requireRole)
- Validation (validateBody, validateParams)
- Rate limiting (rateLimiter)
- File handling (upload.single)

Store as array in properties: \`"middleware": ["requireAuth", "requireRole", "validateBody"]\`

### Relationship to oRPC Scanner
When both scanners run, Abacus will have two Route nodes for the same endpoint — one from oRPC (contract) and one from Express (runtime). The CLI scan command should detect these overlaps and create \`relates_to\` edges between them. This is handled by the SyncScanResults logic in the Go core, not by this scanner.

## Testing

- [ ] Parses router.get/post/put/delete → correct Route nodes
- [ ] Handles parameterized routes (:id, :slug)
- [ ] Extracts middleware chain names
- [ ] Detects app.use() prefix mounting and composes full paths
- [ ] Handles router.route() chaining
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly Express route files

## Validation

- [ ] All 30+ hourly Express route files scanned
- [ ] Middleware chains captured (reveals auth/validation requirements)
- [ ] Route mounting prefixes correctly composed
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output

## Files

- scanners/express/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/express/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/express/__tests__/

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (Scanner Protocol v1 schema)
title: Phase 2: Express Route Scanner

description:
## What

Design and implement the SQLite database schema and Go type system for the Abacus application graph. Includes all table definitions, indexes, FTS5, and the DB connection module.

## Context

Same SQL schema as before — the database design is language-independent. The Go layer uses modernc.org/sqlite (pure Go, standard database/sql interface). WAL mode for concurrent reads, NORMAL synchronous for CLI speed.

## Implementation

### Go Types (internal/db/types.go)
\`\`\`go
package db

type NodeKind string
const (
    NodeRoute      NodeKind = "route"
    NodeEntity     NodeKind = "entity"
    NodePage       NodeKind = "page"
    NodeAction     NodeKind = "action"
    NodePermission NodeKind = "permission"
)

type NodeSource string
const (
    SourceScan   NodeSource = "scan"
    SourceAgent  NodeSource = "agent"
    SourceManual NodeSource = "manual"
)

type EdgeKind string
const (
    EdgeUsesRoute          EdgeKind = "uses_route"
    EdgeTouchesEntity      EdgeKind = "touches_entity"
    EdgeOnPage             EdgeKind = "on_page"
    EdgeRequiresPermission EdgeKind = "requires_permission"
    EdgeRelatesTo          EdgeKind = "relates_to"
    EdgeFieldRelation      EdgeKind = "field_relation"
)

type GraphNode struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    Kind       NodeKind       \`json:"kind"\`
    Name       string         \`json:"name"\`
    Label      string         \`json:"label"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    Source     NodeSource     \`json:"source"\`
    SourceFile *string        \`json:"source_file"\`
    CreatedAt  int64          \`json:"created_at"\`
    UpdatedAt  int64          \`json:"updated_at"\`
    ScanHash   *string        \`json:"scan_hash"\`
}

type GraphEdge struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    SrcID      string         \`json:"src_id"\`
    DstID      string         \`json:"dst_id"\`
    Kind       EdgeKind       \`json:"kind"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    CreatedAt  int64          \`json:"created_at"\`
}
\`\`\`

### DB Connection (internal/db/db.go)
- OpenDB(path string) (*sql.DB, error) — open with WAL mode, foreign keys, cache
- InitSchema(db *sql.DB) error — create tables, indexes, FTS5, triggers
- CloseDB(db *sql.DB) error — graceful close
- Properties stored as JSON TEXT (modernc.org/sqlite supports json_extract)

### SQL Schema
Same as original plan — nodes, edges, nodes_fts, triggers, schema_version. See epic description for full DDL.

## Testing

- [ ] OpenDB creates database with correct pragmas
- [ ] InitSchema creates all tables and indexes
- [ ] Node CRUD operations work for all 5 kinds
- [ ] Edge CRUD with UNIQUE constraint enforcement
- [ ] FTS5 search with BM25 ranking
- [ ] JSON properties round-trip correctly
- [ ] Cascading edge deletion on node delete
- [ ] Schema versioning works
- [ ] Transaction rollback on error

## Validation

- [ ] All 5 node types stored and retrieved correctly
- [ ] Edges maintain referential integrity
- [ ] FTS5 returns relevant results
- [ ] Pure Go — no CGo compilation required

## Files

- internal/db/types.go
- internal/db/db.go
- internal/db/schema.go (DDL strings)
- internal/db/db_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.1 (Go module must exist)
title: Phase 1: Core Types & SQLite Schema

description:
## What

Design and implement the Scanner Protocol — the BYOASTP (Bring Your Own AST Parser) interface that defines how external scanner executables communicate with Abacus. This is the most architecturally important bead: it defines the contract that makes scanners language-agnostic.

## Context

Abacus does NOT parse source code. Instead, scanners are standalone executables that:
1. Receive configuration via stdin (JSON)
2. Parse source files in whatever language they understand
3. Output a ScanResult JSON document to stdout
4. Exit 0 on success, non-zero on failure (errors to stderr)

The Scanner Protocol is a versioned JSON schema. Abacus ships v1; future versions maintain backward compatibility.

## Implementation

### Scanner Protocol v1 — The Interface

**Input (stdin → scanner):**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "projectRoot": "/absolute/path/to/project",
  "options": {
    "contractPaths": ["shared/api-client/src/contracts/**/*.ts"]
  }
}
\`\`\`

**Output (scanner → stdout):**
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "scanner": {
    "id": "orpc",
    "name": "oRPC Route Scanner",
    "version": "0.1.0"
  },
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "route:POST-/api/auth/register",
      "kind": "route",
      "name": "POST /api/auth/register",
      "label": "Register a new user",
      "properties": {
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/api/auth/register",
        "summary": "Register a new user",
        "tags": ["Auth"],
        "inputSchema": "RegisterInputSchema",
        "outputSchema": "AuthResponseSchema",
        "successStatus": 201,
        "contractFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts",
        "handlerFile": "backend/src/orpc/auth.handlers.ts"
      },
      "source": "scan",
      "sourceFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
    }
  ],
  "edges": [
    {
      "id": "edge:entity:User-field_relation-entity:Company",
      "srcId": "entity:User",
      "dstId": "entity:Company",
      "kind": "field_relation",
      "properties": {
        "fieldName": "company",
        "relationType": "many-to-one"
      }
    }
  ],
  "warnings": [
    {
      "file": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/broken.ts",
      "line": 42,
      "message": "Failed to parse: unexpected token",
      "severity": "warn"
    }
  ],
  "stats": {
    "filesScanned": 45,
    "nodesFound": 47,
    "edgesFound": 12,
    "errors": 1,
    "durationMs": 1234
  }
}
\`\`\`

### Go Types for Protocol (internal/scanner/protocol.go)

\`\`\`go
package scanner

// ScanInput is sent to scanner executables via stdin
type ScanInput struct {
    Version     int            \`json:"version"\`
    ProjectRoot string         \`json:"projectRoot"\`
    Options     map[string]any \`json:"options"\`
}

// ScanOutput is received from scanner executables via stdout
type ScanOutput struct {
    Version  int            \`json:"version"\`
    Scanner  ScannerInfo    \`json:"scanner"\`
    Nodes    []ScanNode     \`json:"nodes"\`
    Edges    []ScanEdge     \`json:"edges"\`
    Warnings []ScanWarning  \`json:"warnings"\`
    Stats    ScanStats      \`json:"stats"\`
}

type ScannerInfo struct {
    ID      string \`json:"id"\`
    Name    string \`json:"name"\`
    Version string \`json:"version"\`
}

type ScanNode struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    Kind       string         \`json:"kind"\`   // must be valid NodeKind
    Name       string         \`json:"name"\`
    Label      string         \`json:"label"\`
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties"\`
    Source     string         \`json:"source"\` // always "scan" for scanners
    SourceFile string         \`json:"sourceFile,omitempty"\`
}

type ScanEdge struct {
    ID         string         \`json:"id"\`
    SrcID      string         \`json:"srcId"\`
    DstID      string         \`json:"dstId"\`
    Kind       string         \`json:"kind"\`   // must be valid EdgeKind
    Properties map[string]any \`json:"properties,omitempty"\`
}

type ScanWarning struct {
    File     string \`json:"file"\`
    Line     *int   \`json:"line,omitempty"\`
    Message  string \`json:"message"\`
    Severity string \`json:"severity"\` // "info", "warn", "error"
}

type ScanStats struct {
    FilesScanned int   \`json:"filesScanned"\`
    NodesFound   int   \`json:"nodesFound"\`
    EdgesFound   int   \`json:"edgesFound"\`
    Errors       int   \`json:"errors"\`
    DurationMs   int64 \`json:"durationMs"\`
}
\`\`\`

### Scanner Runner (internal/scanner/runner.go)

\`\`\`go
package scanner

// Runner spawns scanner executables and collects results
type Runner struct {
    timeout time.Duration  // default: 60s
}

// RunScanner spawns a scanner executable, sends input via stdin, reads output from stdout
func (r *Runner) RunScanner(ctx context.Context, command string, input ScanInput) (*ScanOutput, error)

// RunAll runs all configured scanners (sequentially or parallel) and merges results
func (r *Runner) RunAll(ctx context.Context, configs []ScannerConfig) (*MergedScanOutput, error)

// ValidateOutput checks that scanner output conforms to protocol v1
func ValidateOutput(output *ScanOutput) []string  // returns validation errors
\`\`\`

**RunScanner implementation:**
1. Parse command string into executable + args
2. Create exec.CommandContext with timeout
3. Pipe ScanInput JSON to stdin
4. Capture stdout (ScanOutput JSON) and stderr (error messages)
5. Parse and validate the JSON output
6. Return ScanOutput or error with stderr contents

**Error handling:**
- Scanner exits non-zero → return error with stderr
- Scanner produces invalid JSON → return parse error with raw output
- Scanner times out → kill process, return timeout error
- Scanner outputs valid JSON with warnings → return output + log warnings

### Protocol Validation Rules
- version must be 1
- scanner.id must be non-empty
- All nodes must have id, kind, name, label
- kind must be one of: route, entity, page, action, permission
- All edges must reference node IDs that exist in the nodes array
- Edge kind must be valid EdgeKind
- No duplicate node IDs

### JSON Schema File
Generate a JSON Schema file (protocol/scanner-v1.schema.json) that scanner authors can validate against. This serves as documentation AND validation tool.

## Testing

- [ ] RunScanner spawns a test executable and reads its output
- [ ] RunScanner sends ScanInput JSON via stdin correctly
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner exit code 0 → success
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner exit code 1 → error with stderr
- [ ] RunScanner handles scanner timeout → kills process
- [ ] RunScanner handles invalid JSON output → clear error message
- [ ] ValidateOutput accepts valid ScanOutput
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects output with missing required fields
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects output with invalid node kinds
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects edges referencing nonexistent nodes
- [ ] ValidateOutput rejects duplicate node IDs
- [ ] RunAll runs multiple scanners and merges results
- [ ] RunAll continues when one scanner fails, reports error in warnings
- [ ] ScanInput JSON serialization is correct
- [ ] ScanOutput JSON deserialization handles all field types
- [ ] Test with a simple bash scanner: \`echo '{"version":1,...}'\`

## Validation

- [ ] Protocol is simple enough that a bash one-liner can be a valid scanner
- [ ] Protocol JSON schema is valid and documents all fields
- [ ] Runner handles real-world edge cases (large output, slow scanners)
- [ ] No Go-specific assumptions in the protocol (truly language-agnostic)
- [ ] Merged output from multiple scanners has no duplicate nodes

## Files

- internal/scanner/protocol.go (types)
- internal/scanner/runner.go (subprocess execution)
- internal/scanner/validate.go (output validation)
- internal/scanner/runner_test.go
- internal/scanner/validate_test.go
- protocol/scanner-v1.schema.json (JSON Schema for scanner authors)
- testdata/scanners/echo-scanner.sh (test fixture)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.2 (needs db types for NodeKind, EdgeKind validation)
title: Phase 1: Pluggable Scanner Architecture

description:
## What

Implement the oRPC Route Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-orpc) that parses oRPC contract files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON.

## Context

This is a BYOASTP scanner — a standalone Node.js executable that:
1. Reads ScanInput JSON from stdin (projectRoot, options.contractPaths, options.handlerPaths)
2. Uses ts-morph to parse TypeScript AST and extract route definitions
3. Outputs ScanOutput JSON to stdout conforming to Scanner Protocol v1
4. Exits 0 on success

The target codebase has 45+ oRPC contracts like:
\`\`\`typescript
export const registerAuth = oc
  .route({ method: "POST", path: "/auth/register", summary: "Register a new user", tags: ["Auth"], successStatus: 201 })
  .input(RegisterInputSchema)
  .output(AuthResponseSchema);
\`\`\`

## Implementation

### Package Structure (scanners/orpc/)
\`\`\`
scanners/orpc/
├── package.json       # name: @abacus/scanner-orpc, bin: { abacus-scanner-orpc: "./dist/index.js" }
├── tsconfig.json
├── src/
│   ├── index.ts       # Entry point: read stdin, parse, write stdout
│   └── parser.ts      # ts-morph AST parsing logic
└── __tests__/
    └── parser.test.ts
\`\`\`

### Entry Point (src/index.ts)
\`\`\`typescript
#!/usr/bin/env node
import { readFileSync } from 'fs';

// Read ScanInput from stdin
const input = JSON.parse(readFileSync('/dev/stdin', 'utf8'));

// Parse contracts
const result = await parseOrpcContracts(input.projectRoot, input.options);

// Write ScanOutput to stdout
console.log(JSON.stringify(result));
\`\`\`

### Parser (src/parser.ts)
- Use ts-morph to find .route() call expressions
- Extract method, path, summary, tags, successStatus from object literal
- Follow chain to .input() and .output() to get schema names
- Generate node ID: \`route:{METHOD}-{path}\`
- Compute scan hash per file (SHA-256 of file content)

### Output Example
\`\`\`json
{
  "version": 1,
  "scanner": { "id": "orpc", "name": "oRPC Route Scanner", "version": "0.1.0" },
  "nodes": [
    {
      "id": "route:POST-/api/auth/register",
      "kind": "route",
      "name": "POST /api/auth/register",
      "label": "Register a new user",
      "properties": {
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/api/auth/register",
        "summary": "Register a new user",
        "tags": ["Auth"],
        "inputSchema": "RegisterInputSchema",
        "outputSchema": "AuthResponseSchema",
        "successStatus": 201,
        "contractFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
      },
      "source": "scan",
      "sourceFile": "shared/api-client/src/contracts/auth.ts"
    }
  ],
  "edges": [],
  "warnings": [],
  "stats": { "filesScanned": 45, "nodesFound": 47, "edgesFound": 0, "errors": 0, "durationMs": 1500 }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] Parses simple .route() call → correct node
- [ ] Handles all HTTP methods (POST, GET, PUT, DELETE, PATCH)
- [ ] Extracts tags, summary, successStatus
- [ ] Extracts input/output schema names
- [ ] Handles contracts without input or output
- [ ] Handles multiple contracts per file
- [ ] Generates deterministic IDs
- [ ] Skips files with syntax errors, reports warning
- [ ] Reads stdin, writes valid ScanOutput JSON to stdout
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly contract files via fixtures
- [ ] Conforms to Scanner Protocol v1 schema

## Validation

- [ ] All 45+ hourly contracts parsed successfully
- [ ] Output is valid Scanner Protocol v1 JSON
- [ ] \`echo '{"version":1,"projectRoot":"/path","options":{...}}' | npx @abacus/scanner-orpc\` works

## Files

- scanners/orpc/package.json
- scanners/orpc/tsconfig.json
- scanners/orpc/src/index.ts
- scanners/orpc/src/parser.ts
- scanners/orpc/__tests__/parser.test.ts
- scanners/orpc/__tests__/fixtures/ (contract fixtures)

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (must know Scanner Protocol v1 schema)
title: Phase 2: oRPC Route Scanner

description:
## What

Implement the Prisma Entity Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-prisma) that parses Prisma schema files and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON.

## Context

BYOASTP scanner. Reads ScanInput from stdin, uses @prisma/internals getDMMF() to parse the schema, outputs ScanOutput JSON to stdout. The target codebase has 100+ Prisma models.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/prisma/
- Entry point reads stdin, calls getDMMF, transforms DMMF to ScanOutput
- Each model → Entity node with fields and relations in properties
- Each enum → Entity node with isEnum=true
- Model-to-model relations → field_relation edges
- Node ID: \`entity:{ModelName}\`

### Relation Edge Generation
For each Prisma @relation, create an edge:
\`\`\`json
{
  "id": "edge:entity:User-field_relation-entity:Company",
  "srcId": "entity:User",
  "dstId": "entity:Company",
  "kind": "field_relation",
  "properties": { "fieldName": "company", "relationType": "many-to-one" }
}
\`\`\`

## Testing

- [ ] Parses simple model with scalar fields → correct Entity node
- [ ] Extracts field attributes (required, id, unique, default)
- [ ] Identifies relations and creates edges
- [ ] Handles 1:1, 1:N, M:N relations
- [ ] Extracts enums with isEnum=true
- [ ] Handles @@map table name overrides
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly Prisma schema

## Validation

- [ ] All 100+ hourly models parsed with correct relations
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output
- [ ] Scan completes in < 3 seconds

## Files

- scanners/prisma/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/prisma/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/prisma/__tests__/

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (Scanner Protocol v1 schema)
title: Phase 2: Prisma Entity Scanner

description:
## What

Implement the React Router Page Scanner as a standalone Node.js package (@abacus/scanner-react-router) that parses React Router configuration and outputs Scanner Protocol v1 JSON.

## Context

BYOASTP scanner. Uses ts-morph to parse JSX <Route> elements from React Router config files. The target codebase uses React Router DOM with JSX Routes in frontend/src/App.tsx.

## Implementation

### Package: scanners/react-router/
- Parse <Route path="..." element={<Component />} /> JSX elements
- Extract path, component name, protection status (auth guards)
- Track nesting hierarchy for layout routes
- Node ID: \`page:{urlPattern}\`

### Protection Detection
Look for auth conditional patterns:
\`\`\`tsx
{user ? <MainApp /> : <Navigate to="/login" />}
\`\`\`
Routes inside protected wrappers get isProtected=true.

## Testing

- [ ] Parses <Route path="/foo" element={<Foo />} /> → correct Page node
- [ ] Handles dynamic segments (:id)
- [ ] Detects protected routes (auth conditionals)
- [ ] Handles catch-all routes (/*)
- [ ] Tracks nesting hierarchy
- [ ] Warns about routes in separate files it can't follow
- [ ] Output conforms to Scanner Protocol v1
- [ ] Integration: parses actual hourly App.tsx

## Validation

- [ ] All top-level hourly routes captured
- [ ] Protected vs public correctly detected
- [ ] Valid Scanner Protocol v1 output

## Files

- scanners/react-router/package.json, tsconfig.json
- scanners/react-router/src/index.ts, src/parser.ts
- scanners/react-router/__tests__/

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.3 (Scanner Protocol v1 schema)
title: Phase 2: React Router Page Scanner
Phase 3: Graph Query Layer

description:
## What

Implement Action CRUD and the enrichment protocol in Go. Actions are the semantic bridge between Gherkin steps and technical graph nodes.

## Context

Same design as original — ActionService wraps GraphRepository with validation, reference resolution, and the enrichment protocol. Now in Go.

## Implementation

### ActionService (internal/graph/actions.go)
\`\`\`go
type ActionService struct {
    repo *GraphRepository
}

func (s *ActionService) Create(input CreateActionInput) (*ActionResult, error)
func (s *ActionService) Update(id string, input UpdateActionInput) (*ActionResult, error)
func (s *ActionService) Get(id string) (*ActionNode, error)
func (s *ActionService) List(opts ListActionOpts) ([]ActionNode, error)
func (s *ActionService) Delete(id string) error
func (s *ActionService) Suggest(ctx SuggestContext) ([]ActionNode, error)
func (s *ActionService) Audit() (*ActionAudit, error)
\`\`\`

### Validation
- Gherkin patterns validated via cucumber-expressions-go (must parse)
- Route/Entity/Page references validated against graph (warn if missing)
- Action name uniqueness enforced
- Creates edges: uses_route, touches_entity, on_page

## Testing

- [ ] Create with valid references → success with edges
- [ ] Create with missing references → success with warnings
- [ ] Create with invalid cucumber expression → error
- [ ] Update merges gherkinPatterns
- [ ] Audit detects stale references
- [ ] Reference resolution populates related nodes

## Files

- internal/graph/actions.go
- internal/graph/actions_test.go

## Dependencies

Depends on: aba-zca.7 (needs GraphRepository)
title: Phase 3: Action CRUD & Enrichment Protocol

description:
## What

Implement the tiered match algorithm in Go — the core feature that makes BDD work. Three tiers: Cucumber expression match → FTS5 fuzzy match → LLM-assisted Action creation suggestion.

## Context

Uses cucumber-expressions-go for Tier 1 (exact matching with parameter extraction), SQLite FTS5 for Tier 2 (BM25 ranked fuzzy search), and structured context packaging for Tier 3 (suggestion builder).

Tier 3 is the GROWTH mechanism: when no Action matches, the algorithm queries the graph for related Routes/Entities/Pages and packages a CreateActionInput suggestion. The LLM (Claude via MCP) reviews and persists it. The graph learns from every new feature.

## Implementation

### MatchService (internal/match/matcher.go)
\`\`\`go
type MatchService struct {
    repo    *graph.GraphRepository
    actions *graph.ActionService
    opts    MatchOptions
}

func (m *MatchService) Match(stepText string, keyword *string) *MatchResult
func (m *MatchService) MatchScenario(steps []Step) []MatchResult
\`\`\`

### MatchResult
\`\`\`go
type MatchResult struct {
    Tier       string          // "exact", "fuzzy", "suggest"
    Action     *ActionNode     // Tier 1
    Parameters map[string]string // Tier 1 extracted params
    Candidates []FuzzyCandidate  // Tier 2
    Suggestion *CreateActionInput // Tier 3
    Context    *RelatedContext   // Tier 3
}
\`\`\`

### CucumberMatcher (internal/match/cucumber.go)
- Uses cucumber-expressions-go to compile patterns and match step text
- Expression caching for performance
- Parameter extraction from matched steps

### FuzzyMatcher (internal/match/fuzzy.go)
- Tokenizes step text (removes Given/When/Then, articles)
- Queries FTS5 with BM25 ranking
- Threshold filtering (configurable, default 0.3)

### SuggestionBuilder (internal/match/suggest.go)
- Extracts keywords from step text
- Queries graph for related nodes (routes, entities, pages matching keywords)
- Generates suggested Action name and Cucumber expression patterns
- Packages everything as CreateActionInput + RelatedContext

## Testing

### Tier 1
- [ ] Exact match with parameter extraction
- [ ] No match returns nil (falls through)
- [ ] Multiple patterns per Action — matches on any
- [ ] Expression caching works

### Tier 2
- [ ] Fuzzy match finds similar Actions
- [ ] BM25 ranking orders by relevance
- [ ] Threshold filtering excludes low-confidence results

### Tier 3
- [ ] Extracts keywords from step text
- [ ] Finds related graph nodes
- [ ] Generates valid CreateActionInput suggestion
- [ ] Always returns something (never empty)

### Integration
- [ ] Tier cascade: exact → fuzzy → suggest
- [ ] MatchScenario processes multiple steps
- [ ] Performance: 100 Actions matched in < 50ms
